---
title: "Python Data Structures & Algorithms"
subtitle: "A Complete Visual Guide"
author: "DSA with Python"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [dark, custom.scss]
    highlight-style: monokai
    filters:
      - code-fullscreen
    transition: slide
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: false
    footer: "Python DSA — Complete Guide"
    progress: true
    code-fold: false
    code-line-numbers: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    incremental: false
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.05
    center: false
  pptx:
    mermaid-format: png
mermaid:
  theme: dark

execute:
  echo: false
  eval: true
---

## What is DSA? {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
**Data Structures** are ways to **organise and store data** so it can be accessed and modified efficiently.

**Algorithms** are step-by-step **procedures** to solve problems using that data.

<br>

::: {.fragment}
Together they answer two questions:

- **How** should data be stored?
- **How** should data be processed?
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
Mastering DSA is the foundation for writing **efficient, scalable software** and **acing technical interviews**.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    DSA["🧠 DSA"] --> DS["📦 Data Structures"]
    DSA --> AL["⚙️ Algorithms"]
    DS --> Linear["Linear<br/>Array · List · Stack · Queue"]
    DS --> NonLinear["Non-Linear<br/>Tree · Graph · Heap"]
    DS --> Hashed["Hashed<br/>Hash Table · Set"]
    AL --> Search["Searching<br/>Linear · Binary"]
    AL --> Sort["Sorting<br/>Merge · Quick · Heap"]
    AL --> Graph["Graph<br/>BFS · DFS · Dijkstra"]
    AL --> DP["Optimisation<br/>DP · Greedy"]
    classDef root  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef cat   fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef leaf  fill:#16213e,stroke:#555,color:#ccc
    class DSA root
    class DS,AL cat
    class Linear,NonLinear,Hashed,Search,Sort,Graph,DP leaf
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Why DSA Matters {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
### Performance Gap

| Input Size | O(n²) ops | O(n log n) ops |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | 10,000 | 665 |
| 1,000 | 1,000,000 | 9,966 |
| 1,000,000 | 10¹² | ~20,000,000 |
:::

::: {.fragment}
A naive O(n²) sort on **1 million items** at 10⁹ ops/sec takes **~17 minutes**.  
An O(n log n) sort takes **~0.02 seconds**.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
### Real-world Impact

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    G["Google Search"] --> BFS["BFS / PageRank"]
    G2["Google Maps"] --> DJ["Dijkstra's Algorithm"]
    FB["Social Networks"] --> GR["Graph Traversal"]
    DB["Databases"] --> BT["B-trees & Hash Tables"]
    AI["AI / ML"] --> DP["Dynamic Programming"]
    classDef app  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef algo fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class G,G2,FB,DB,AI app
    class BFS,DJ,GR,BT,DP algo
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Big-O Notation {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Big-O** describes how runtime or memory **scales** as input size *n* grows — it gives a **worst-case upper bound**.

<br>

::: {.fragment}
| Notation | Name | Example |
|---|---|---|
| O(1) | Constant | Array index access |
| O(log n) | Logarithmic | Binary search |
| O(n) | Linear | Linear search |
| O(n log n) | Linearithmic | Merge sort |
| O(n²) | Quadratic | Bubble sort |
| O(2ⁿ) | Exponential | Fib (naive) |
| O(n!) | Factorial | Permutations |
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    O1["O(1) ✅ Excellent"]
    OLN["O(log n) ✅ Good"]
    ON["O(n) 🟡 Fair"]
    ONLN["O(n log n) 🟡 Fair"]
    ON2["O(n²) 🔴 Bad"]
    O2N["O(2ⁿ) 💀 Horrible"]
    O1 --> OLN --> ON --> ONLN --> ON2 --> O2N
    classDef good  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef ok    fill:#7a6000,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef bad   fill:#7a1a1a,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class O1,OLN good
    class ON,ONLN ok
    class ON2,O2N bad
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
Drop constants and lower-order terms: **O(3n² + 2n + 5) → O(n²)**
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Data Structures Roadmap {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'flowchart': {'rankSpacing': 60, 'nodeSpacing': 40}}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph Linear["⬜ Linear"]
        ARR["Array / List"]
        LL["Linked List"]
        ST["Stack"]
        QU["Queue"]
        DQ["Deque"]
    end
    subgraph Hashed["🔷 Hashed"]
        HT["Hash Table<br/>(dict)"]
        HS["Hash Set<br/>(set)"]
    end
    subgraph Trees["🌲 Trees"]
        BT["Binary Tree"]
        BST["BST"]
        HP["Heap"]
        TR["Trie"]
    end
    subgraph Graphs["🕸 Graphs"]
        DG["Directed"]
        UG["Undirected"]
        WG["Weighted"]
    end
    classDef lin  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef hash fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    classDef tree fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#f5a623,color:#fff
    classDef grph fill:#0d0d1a,stroke:#a29bfe,color:#fff
    class ARR,LL,ST,QU,DQ lin
    class HT,HS hash
    class BT,BST,HP,TR tree
    class DG,UG,WG grph
```

::: {.fragment}
> **Choosing a data structure** is the first — and most important — design decision.
:::

---

## Arrays — Memory Layout {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
An **array** stores elements in **contiguous memory** — all elements are back-to-back, enabling **O(1) random access**.

<!-- $$address(i) = base + i \times element\_size$$ -->

$$address(i) = base + i \times element\_size$$


::: {.fragment}
### Python `list` — Dynamic Array

```python
nums = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
print(nums[2])      # O(1) → 30
nums.append(60)     # O(1) amortised
nums.insert(0, 5)   # O(n) — shifts all
nums.pop()          # O(1)
nums.pop(0)         # O(n) — shifts all
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "RAM — contiguous blocks"
        direction LR
        A0["[0]<br/>10"] --- A1["[1]<br/>20"] --- A2["[2]<br/>30"] --- A3["[3]<br/>40"] --- A4["[4]<br/>50"]
    end
    P0(["base"]) -.-> A0
    P2(["base+8"]) -.-> A2
    classDef cell fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef ptr  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4 cell
    class P0,P2 ptr
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Complexity

| Operation | Time |
|---|---|
| Access by index | O(1) |
| Search (unsorted) | O(n) |
| Insert at end | O(1)* |
| Insert at front | O(n) |
| Delete at end | O(1) |
| Delete at front | O(n) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Linked Lists — Structure {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **linked list** stores nodes in **non-contiguous** memory. Each node holds **data** and a **pointer** to the next node.

::: {.fragment}
```python
class Node:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.next = None   # pointer

class LinkedList:
    def __init__(self):
        self.head = None

    def prepend(self, data):        # O(1)
        node = Node(data)
        node.next = self.head
        self.head = node

    def append(self, data):         # O(n)
        node = Node(data)
        if not self.head:
            self.head = node; return
        curr = self.head
        while curr.next:
            curr = curr.next
        curr.next = node
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    HEAD(["HEAD"]) --> N1
    N1["10 | ●"] --> N2["20 | ●"] --> N3["30 | ∅"]
    N3 --> NIL(["None"])
    classDef h   fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef n   fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef nil fill:#333,stroke:none,color:#aaa
    class HEAD h
    class N1,N2,N3 n
    class NIL nil
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Complexity

| Operation | Time |
|---|---|
| Prepend | O(1) |
| Append | O(n) |
| Search | O(n) |
| Delete head | O(1) |
| Delete node | O(n) |
| Access by index | O(n) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Arrays vs Linked Lists {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    Q["Need fast random access?"]
    Q --> |Yes| ARR["Use Array / List<br/>O(1) index access"]
    Q --> |No| Q2["Frequent front insertions/<br/>deletions?"]
    Q2 --> |Yes| LL["Use Linked List<br/>O(1) prepend"]
    Q2 --> |No| Q3["Need LIFO or FIFO<br/>behaviour?"]
    Q3 --> |LIFO| ST["Use Stack<br/>(list / deque)"]
    Q3 --> |FIFO| QU["Use Queue<br/>(collections.deque)"]
    Q3 --> |No| ARR2["Use Array by default"]
    classDef q   fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef ans fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class Q,Q2,Q3 q
    class ARR,ARR2,LL,ST,QU ans
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
| Feature | Array | Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Contiguous | Scattered |
| Access | O(1) | O(n) |
| Prepend | O(n) | **O(1)** |
| Append | O(1)* | O(n) |
| Delete head | O(n) | **O(1)** |
| Cache perf. | Excellent | Poor |
| Extra memory | None | 1–2 pointers |

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
Python's built-in `collections.deque` gives **O(1) append and prepend** — best of both worlds for queue/stack use cases.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Stacks — LIFO {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **stack** is a **Last In, First Out (LIFO)** structure — the last element pushed is the first popped.

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Python list as a stack
stack = []
stack.append(10)    # push  → [10]
stack.append(20)    # push  → [10, 20]
stack.append(30)    # push  → [10, 20, 30]

top = stack[-1]     # peek  → 30  (O(1))
stack.pop()         # pop   → 30  (O(1))
print(stack)        # [10, 20]
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Use Cases
- Function **call stack** (recursion)
- **Undo / Redo** in editors
- **Bracket matching** (valid parentheses)
- **Expression evaluation** (postfix/infix)
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TB
    subgraph "Stack"
        direction TB
        TOP(["▶ TOP"]) --> S3["30  ← last pushed"]
        S3 --> S2["20"]
        S2 --> S1["10  ← first pushed"]
        S1 --> BOT(["⊥ BOTTOM"])
    end
    PUSH(["push(40)"]) -.->|insert here| S3
    POP(["pop() → 30"]) -.->|remove here| S3
    classDef top  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef cell fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef act  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class TOP,BOT top
    class S1,S2,S3 cell
    class PUSH,POP act
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Operation | Time |
|---|---|
| push | O(1) |
| pop | O(1) |
| peek | O(1) |
| search | O(n) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Stack — Bracket Matching {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A classic stack application — verify every opening bracket has a matching closing bracket.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-4|6-13|15-17"}
def is_valid(s: str) -> bool:
    stack = []
    pairs = {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}

    for ch in s:
        if ch in '([{':
            stack.append(ch)          # push opening
        elif ch in ')]}':
            if not stack or stack[-1] != pairs[ch]:
                return False          # mismatch
            stack.pop()               # matched — pop

    return len(stack) == 0            # empty ⇒ valid

print(is_valid("({[]})"))  # True
print(is_valid("({[})"))   # False
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
sequenceDiagram
    participant Input
    participant Stack
    Input->>Stack: push '('
    Input->>Stack: push '{'
    Input->>Stack: push '['
    Input->>Stack: pop '[' ← match ']'
    Input->>Stack: pop '{' ← match '}'
    Input->>Stack: pop '(' ← match ')'
    Note over Stack: Empty → Valid ✅
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Queues — FIFO {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **queue** is a **First In, First Out (FIFO)** structure — the first element added is the first removed.

::: {.fragment}
```python
from collections import deque

q = deque()
q.append(10)        # enqueue  → deque([10])
q.append(20)        # enqueue  → deque([10, 20])
q.append(30)        # enqueue  → deque([10, 20, 30])

front = q[0]        # peek     → 10  (O(1))
q.popleft()         # dequeue  → 10  (O(1))
print(q)            # deque([20, 30])
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Use Cases
- **BFS** (breadth-first search)
- **Task scheduling** (OS process queues)
- **Print spooling**
- **Web server** request handling
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    EN(["enqueue ➡"]) -->|rear| Q1["10"]
    Q1 --> Q2["20"]
    Q2 --> Q3["30"]
    Q3 -->|front| DE(["➡ dequeue"])
    classDef end_ fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef cell fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class EN,DE end_
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 cell
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Variants

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| Simple Queue | FIFO |
| Circular Queue | ring buffer |
| Priority Queue | sorted by priority |
| Deque | insert / remove both ends |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Hash Tables — How Hashing Works {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **hash table** maps **keys → values** using a **hash function**:

$$index = hash(key) \mod capacity$$

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Python dict is a hash table
d = {}
d["Alice"] = "555-1234"   # insert O(1)
d["Bob"]   = "555-5678"

print(d["Alice"])          # lookup O(1)
"Alice" in d               # membership O(1)
del d["Bob"]               # delete O(1)
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Collision Resolution

| Strategy | Idea |
|---|---|
| Chaining | Linked list per bucket |
| Open addressing | Probe next free slot |
| Robin Hood | Steal from rich slots |
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    K1(["'Alice'"]) --> HF["hash()"]
    K2(["'Bob'"])   --> HF
    K3(["'Carol'"]) --> HF
    HF --> B2["[2] → 555-1234"]
    HF --> B5["[5] → 555-5678"]
    HF --> B7["[7] → 555-4321"]
    B2 -->|collision| CH["[2]→ chain:<br/>555-1234<br/>555-9999"]
    classDef k   fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef hf  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef b   fill:#16213e,stroke:#555,color:#eee
    class K1,K2,K3 k
    class HF hf
    class B2,B5,B7,CH b
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Operation | Average | Worst |
|---|---|---|
| Insert | O(1) | O(n) |
| Lookup | O(1) | O(n) |
| Delete | O(1) | O(n) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Trees — Core Concepts {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
A **tree** is a hierarchical, non-linear data structure consisting of **nodes** connected by **edges**, with no cycles.

::: {.fragment}
Key terminology:

- **Root** — top-most node (no parent)
- **Leaf** — node with no children
- **Height** — longest path root → leaf
- **Depth** — distance from root to node
- **Subtree** — tree rooted at any node
:::

::: {.fragment}
```python
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val   = val
        self.left  = None
        self.right = None
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    ROOT["1<br/>ROOT<br/>depth=0"] --> N2["2<br/>depth=1"]
    ROOT --> N3["3<br/>depth=1"]
    N2   --> N4["4<br/>LEAF"]
    N2   --> N5["5<br/>LEAF"]
    N3   --> N6["6<br/>LEAF"]
    N3   --> N7["7<br/>LEAF"]
    classDef root fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    class ROOT root
    class N2,N3 node
    class N4,N5,N6,N7 leaf
```

Height = **2** · Leaves = **4** · Nodes = **7**
:::
:::
:::

---

## Binary Search Tree (BST) {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **BST** enforces an ordering property on every node:

> **left subtree < node < right subtree**

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-8|10-18|20-28"}
class BST:
    def __init__(self):
        self.root = None

    def insert(self, val):
        self.root = self._insert(self.root, val)

    def _insert(self, node, val):
        if node is None:
            return TreeNode(val)
        if val < node.val:
            node.left  = self._insert(node.left, val)
        elif val > node.val:
            node.right = self._insert(node.right, val)
        return node

    def search(self, val) -> bool:
        cur = self.root
        while cur:
            if   val == cur.val: return True
            elif val <  cur.val: cur = cur.left
            else:                cur = cur.right
        return False
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    R["50"] --> L["30"]
    R --> RI["70"]
    L --> LL["20"]
    L --> LR["40"]
    RI --> RL["60"]
    RI --> RR["80"]
    classDef root fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class R root
    class L,RI,LL,LR,RL,RR node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Operation | Average | Worst (skewed) |
|---|---|---|
| Search | O(log n) | O(n) |
| Insert | O(log n) | O(n) |
| Delete | O(log n) | O(n) |

Worst case occurs when tree is **unbalanced** (sorted input).
:::
:::
:::

---

## Tree Traversals {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Three fundamental **depth-first** traversals of a binary tree:

::: {.fragment}
```python
def inorder(node):      # Left → Root → Right
    if node:
        inorder(node.left)
        print(node.val)
        inorder(node.right)

def preorder(node):     # Root → Left → Right
    if node:
        print(node.val)
        preorder(node.left)
        preorder(node.right)

def postorder(node):    # Left → Right → Root
    if node:
        postorder(node.left)
        postorder(node.right)
        print(node.val)
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
**Inorder** traversal of a BST always yields elements in **sorted order**.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    A["4"] --> B["2"]
    A --> C["6"]
    B --> D["1"]
    B --> E["3"]
    C --> F["5"]
    C --> G["7"]
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Traversal | Order | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Inorder | L-R-Root | 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 |
| Preorder | Root-L-R | 4 2 1 3 6 5 7 |
| Postorder | L-R-Root | 1 3 2 5 7 6 4 |
| Level-order | BFS | 4 2 6 1 3 5 7 |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Heaps — Priority Queue {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **heap** is a complete binary tree satisfying the **heap property**:

- **Min-Heap**: parent ≤ children → root is **minimum**
- **Max-Heap**: parent ≥ children → root is **maximum**

::: {.fragment}
```python
import heapq

# Python's heapq is a MIN-heap by default
heap = []
heapq.heappush(heap, 5)
heapq.heappush(heap, 1)
heapq.heappush(heap, 3)

print(heap[0])              # peek min → 1
smallest = heapq.heappop(heap)  # pop → 1

# Max-heap: negate values
heapq.heappush(heap, -10)
biggest = -heapq.heappop(heap)  # → 10
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    R["1<br/>(min)"] --> A["3"]
    R --> B["5"]
    A --> C["7"]
    A --> D["9"]
    B --> E["6"]
    B --> F["11"]
    classDef root fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class R root
    class A,B,C,D,E,F node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Operation | Time |
|---|---|
| Push (heappush) | O(log n) |
| Pop min/max | O(log n) |
| Peek min/max | O(1) |
| Build heap | O(n) |
| Heap sort | O(n log n) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Graphs — Fundamentals {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **graph G = (V, E)** consists of **vertices** (nodes) connected by **edges**.

::: {.fragment}
Types of graphs:

- **Undirected** — edges have no direction
- **Directed (Digraph)** — edges have direction
- **Weighted** — edges carry a cost/weight
- **Cyclic / Acyclic** — may or may not contain cycles
- **DAG** — Directed Acyclic Graph
:::

::: {.fragment}
Representations:

| Type | Space | Edge check | Neighbours |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adjacency Matrix | O(V²) | O(1) | O(V) |
| Adjacency List | O(V+E) | O(deg) | O(deg) |
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    A(["A"]) -->|4| B(["B"])
    A -->|2| C(["C"])
    B -->|5| C
    B -->|10| D(["D"])
    C -->|3| D
    C -->|8| E(["E"])
    D -->|2| E
    classDef v fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E v
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Adjacency list representation
graph = {
    'A': ['B', 'C'],
    'B': ['C', 'D'],
    'C': ['D', 'E'],
    'D': ['E'],
    'E': []
}
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Graph — BFS (Breadth-First Search) {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**BFS** explores the graph **level by level** using a **queue**. Finds **shortest path** in unweighted graphs.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-17|19-21"}
from collections import deque

def bfs(graph, start):
    visited = set()
    queue   = deque([start])
    visited.add(start)
    order   = []

    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()      # dequeue
        order.append(node)

        for neighbour in graph[node]:
            if neighbour not in visited:
                visited.add(neighbour)
                queue.append(neighbour)

    return order

# graph = {'A': ['B','C'], 'B': ['D'], ...}
# bfs(graph, 'A') → ['A','B','C','D','E']
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    A["A<br/>Level 0"] --> B["B<br/>Level 1"]
    A --> C["C<br/>Level 1"]
    B --> D["D<br/>Level 2"]
    B --> E["E<br/>Level 2"]
    C --> F["F<br/>Level 2"]
    classDef l0 fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef l1 fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef l2 fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    class A l0
    class B,C l1
    class D,E,F l2
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Time complexity | O(V + E) |
| Space complexity | O(V) |
| Finds shortest path | ✅ (unweighted) |
| Strategy | Queue (FIFO) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Graph — DFS (Depth-First Search) {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**DFS** explores as **deep as possible** along each branch before backtracking, using a **stack** (or recursion).

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-7|9-17|19-22"}
def dfs_recursive(graph, node, visited=None):
    if visited is None:
        visited = set()
    visited.add(node)
    print(node)
    for neighbour in graph[node]:
        if neighbour not in visited:
            dfs_recursive(graph, neighbour, visited)

def dfs_iterative(graph, start):
    visited = set()
    stack   = [start]
    order   = []
    while stack:
        node = stack.pop()
        if node not in visited:
            visited.add(node)
            order.append(node)
            stack.extend(reversed(graph[node]))
    return order
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    A["A"] -->|"① visit"| B["B"]
    B -->|"② go deep"| D["D"]
    D -->|"③ backtrack"| B
    B -->|"④ visit"| E["E"]
    E -->|"⑤ backtrack"| A
    A -->|"⑥ visit"| C["C"]
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### BFS vs DFS

| | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Queue | Stack |
| Shortest path | ✅ | ❌ |
| Memory | O(width) | O(depth) |
| Cycle detect | ✅ | ✅ |
| Topological sort | ❌ | ✅ |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Searching — Linear Search {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Linear search** scans every element from left to right until it finds the target.

- Works on **unsorted** data
- Time: **O(n)** · Space: **O(1)**

::: {.fragment}
```python
def linear_search(arr, target):
    for i, val in enumerate(arr):
        if val == target:
            return i     # found at index i
    return -1            # not found

arr = [5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2, 7]
print(linear_search(arr, 9))   # → 4
print(linear_search(arr, 6))   # → -1
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
Use linear search when the list is **small**, **unsorted**, or you need to find **all occurrences**.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "Scan for target=9"
        direction LR
        A0["5<br/>✗"] --> A1["3<br/>✗"] --> A2["8<br/>✗"] --> A3["1<br/>✗"] --> A4["9<br/>✅"]
    end
    classDef miss fill:#7a1a1a,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef hit  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class A0,A1,A2,A3 miss
    class A4 hit
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Case | Comparisons |
|---|---|
| Best (target at front) | O(1) |
| Average | O(n/2) |
| Worst (not found) | O(n) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Searching — Binary Search {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Binary search** works on **sorted** data, halving the search space each step.

- Time: **O(log n)** · Space: **O(1)**

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-4|5-14|16-18"}
def binary_search(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1

    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid           # found
        elif arr[mid] < target:
            lo = mid + 1         # right half
        else:
            hi = mid - 1         # left half

    return -1                     # not found

arr = [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13]
print(binary_search(arr, 7))     # → 3
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    S0["[1  3  5  7  9  11  13]<br/>mid=7  target=7  ✅ Found!"]
    S1["[1  3  5  7  9  11  13]<br/>mid=7  lo=0 hi=6"]
    S1 --> S0
    classDef step fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef hit  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class S1 step
    class S0 hit
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Halving example — searching in 1,000,000 elements:**

| Step | Search space |
|---|---|
| 1 | 500,000 |
| 5 | 31,250 |
| 10 | 977 |
| 20 | 1 |

Only **20 comparisons** for 1M elements!
:::
:::
:::

---

## Sorting Algorithms — Overview {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'flowchart': {'rankSpacing': 50}}}%%
graph LR
    SORT["Sorting<br/>Algorithms"]
    SORT --> COMP["Comparison-based"]
    SORT --> NCOMP["Non-comparison"]
    COMP --> BUB["Bubble Sort<br/>O(n²)"]
    COMP --> SEL["Selection Sort<br/>O(n²)"]
    COMP --> INS["Insertion Sort<br/>O(n²) / O(n) best"]
    COMP --> MRG["Merge Sort<br/>O(n log n) stable"]
    COMP --> QCK["Quick Sort<br/>O(n log n) avg"]
    COMP --> HPS["Heap Sort<br/>O(n log n)"]
    NCOMP --> CNT["Counting Sort<br/>O(n+k)"]
    NCOMP --> RAD["Radix Sort<br/>O(nk)"]
    classDef root fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef cat  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef algo fill:#16213e,stroke:#555,color:#eee
    class SORT root
    class COMP,NCOMP cat
    class BUB,SEL,INS,MRG,QCK,HPS,CNT,RAD algo
```

::: {.fragment}
| Algorithm | Best | Average | Worst | Space | Stable |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) | ✅ |
| Selection | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) | ❌ |
| Insertion | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) | ✅ |
| Merge | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n) | ✅ |
| Quick | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n²) | O(log n) | ❌ |
| Heap | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(1) | ❌ |
:::

---

## Bubble Sort {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Repeatedly **swap adjacent** elements if they are in the wrong order. Large elements **"bubble up"** to the end.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-4|5-10|12-14"}
def bubble_sort(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    for i in range(n):
        swapped = False
        for j in range(0, n - i - 1):
            if arr[j] > arr[j + 1]:
                arr[j], arr[j+1] = arr[j+1], arr[j]
                swapped = True
        if not swapped:       # already sorted
            break
    return arr

arr = [64, 34, 25, 12, 22]
print(bubble_sort(arr))  # [12, 22, 25, 34, 64]
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
The `swapped` flag gives **O(n) best case** when input is already sorted.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
**Pass-by-pass visualisation:**
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "Pass 3 → sorted tail grows"
        direction LR
        P1["12"] --- P2["22"] --- P3["25"] --- P4["34"] --- P5["64"]
    end
    subgraph "Pass 2"
        direction LR
        Q1["12"] --- Q2["22"] --- Q3["25"] --- Q4["34"] --- Q5["64"]
    end
    subgraph "Pass 1"
        direction LR
        R1["34"] --- R2["25"] --- R3["12"] --- R4["22"] --- R5["64"]
    end
    subgraph "Input"
        direction LR
        S1["64"] --- S2["34"] --- S3["25"] --- S4["12"] --- S5["22"]
    end
    classDef sorted fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef unsort fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 sorted
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 unsort
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Merge Sort — Divide & Conquer {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Divide** the array in half, **recursively sort** each half, then **merge** the two sorted halves.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-20"}
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1:
        return arr
    mid   = len(arr) // 2
    left  = merge_sort(arr[:mid])
    right = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    return merge(left, right)

def merge(left, right):
    result = []
    i = j  = 0
    while i < len(left) and j < len(right):
        if left[i] <= right[j]:
            result.append(left[i]);  i += 1
        else:
            result.append(right[j]); j += 1
    result.extend(left[i:])
    result.extend(right[j:])
    return result
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    A["[38, 27, 43, 3]"] --> B["[38, 27]"]
    A --> C["[43, 3]"]
    B --> D["[38]"]
    B --> E["[27]"]
    C --> F["[43]"]
    C --> G["[3]"]
    D --> DE["[27, 38]"]
    E --> DE
    F --> FG["[3, 43]"]
    G --> FG
    DE --> FINAL["[3, 27, 38, 43]"]
    FG --> FINAL
    classDef div  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef mrg  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G div
    class DE,FG,FINAL mrg
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Always O(n log n)** — predictable
- **Stable** — preserves relative order of equals
- **Extra space** O(n) — not in-place
:::
:::
:::

---

## Quick Sort — Partition & Conquer {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Choose a **pivot**, partition the array so all elements < pivot are left and > pivot are right, then recursively sort each side.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-15"}
def quick_sort(arr, lo=0, hi=None):
    if hi is None:
        hi = len(arr) - 1
    if lo < hi:
        p = partition(arr, lo, hi)
        quick_sort(arr, lo, p - 1)
        quick_sort(arr, p + 1, hi)

def partition(arr, lo, hi):
    pivot = arr[hi]      # last element as pivot
    i = lo - 1
    for j in range(lo, hi):
        if arr[j] <= pivot:
            i += 1
            arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]
    arr[i+1], arr[hi] = arr[hi], arr[i+1]
    return i + 1
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    A["[3, 6, 8, 10, 1, 2, 1]<br/>pivot = 1"] --> L["[1] ≤ pivot"]
    A --> P["[1] = pivot"]
    A --> R["[3,6,8,10,2] > pivot"]
    L --> LL["sort [1]"]
    R --> RR["sort [2,3,6,8,10]"]
    classDef input fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef part  fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    class A input
    class L,P,R,LL,RR part
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Case | Time | Occurs when |
|---|---|---|
| Best | O(n log n) | Perfect pivots |
| Average | O(n log n) | Random input |
| Worst | O(n²) | Sorted input |

::: {.callout-tip}
Use **randomised pivot** to avoid worst case in practice.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Recursion — Core Concepts {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A function is **recursive** if it calls itself. Every recursive solution needs:

1. **Base case** — stops the recursion
2. **Recursive case** — reduces problem toward base case
3. **Progress** — each call must get closer to base case

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Factorial: n! = n × (n-1)!
def factorial(n):
    if n == 0:           # base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)  # recursive case

# Fibonacci
def fib(n):
    if n <= 1:           # base case
        return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)  # recursive case

print(factorial(5))  # 120
print(fib(7))        # 13
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    F5["factorial(5)<br/>= 5 × 24"] --> F4["factorial(4)<br/>= 4 × 6"]
    F4 --> F3["factorial(3)<br/>= 3 × 2"]
    F3 --> F2["factorial(2)<br/>= 2 × 1"]
    F2 --> F1["factorial(1)<br/>= 1 × 1"]
    F1 --> F0["factorial(0)<br/>= 1  ← BASE"]
    classDef calls fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef base fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class F5,F4,F3,F2,F1 calls
    class F0 base
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-warning}
Naive `fib(n)` is **O(2ⁿ)** — exponential! Use **memoisation** or **iteration** instead.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Dynamic Programming — Memoisation {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**DP** solves problems by breaking them into **overlapping subproblems** and storing results to avoid recomputation.

::: {.fragment}
**Two approaches:**

- **Top-down (Memoisation)** — recurse + cache
- **Bottom-up (Tabulation)** — fill table iteratively
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-9|11-18"}
# Top-down: memoised Fibonacci
from functools import lru_cache

@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def fib_memo(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    return fib_memo(n-1) + fib_memo(n-2)

# Bottom-up: tabulated Fibonacci
def fib_tab(n):
    if n <= 1: return n
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[1] = 1
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]
    return dp[n]
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    F5["fib(5)"] --> F4["fib(4)"]
    F5 --> F3a["fib(3) ✅ cached"]
    F4 --> F3b["fib(3) ✅ cached"]
    F4 --> F2a["fib(2) ✅ cached"]
    F3b --> F2b["fib(2) ✅ cached"]
    F3b --> F1a["fib(1)=1"]
    classDef live   fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef cached fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class F5,F4,F3b live
    class F3a,F2a,F2b,F1a cached
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Approach | Time | Space |
|---|---|---|
| Naive recursion | O(2ⁿ) | O(n) stack |
| Memoisation | O(n) | O(n) cache |
| Tabulation | O(n) | O(n) table |
| Optimised tab. | O(n) | **O(1)** |
:::
:::
:::

---

## DP — Classic Problems {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
### 0/1 Knapsack

Given items with weights and values, maximise value within a weight limit.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-15"}
def knapsack(weights, values, W):
    n  = len(weights)
    dp = [[0]*(W+1) for _ in range(n+1)]

    for i in range(1, n+1):
        for w in range(W+1):
            # don't take item i
            dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]
            # take item i (if it fits)
            if weights[i-1] <= w:
                dp[i][w] = max(
                    dp[i][w],
                    values[i-1] + dp[i-1][w - weights[i-1]]
                )
    return dp[n][W]
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
### Longest Common Subsequence

```python
def lcs(s1, s2):
    m, n = len(s1), len(s2)
    dp = [[0]*(n+1) for _ in range(m+1)]
    for i in range(1, m+1):
        for j in range(1, n+1):
            if s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
                dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1
            else:
                dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
    return dp[m][n]

lcs("ABCBDAB", "BDCAB")  # → 4 ("BCAB")
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
**DP recognition clues:** overlapping subproblems, optimal substructure, "min/max/count ways" questions.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Greedy Algorithms {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **greedy algorithm** always picks the **locally optimal** choice at each step, hoping it leads to a global optimum.

::: {.fragment}
**Greedy works when:** the problem has **optimal substructure** and the **greedy choice property**.

Examples:
- **Activity Selection** — pick non-overlapping intervals
- **Coin Change** (canonical coins)
- **Huffman Coding**
- **Prim's / Kruskal's** (MST)
- **Dijkstra's** shortest path
:::

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Coin change with canonical coins (greedy works!)
def coin_change_greedy(coins, amount):
    coins.sort(reverse=True)
    count = 0
    for coin in coins:
        count   += amount // coin
        amount  %= coin
    return count if amount == 0 else -1
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
### Activity Selection
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
gantt
    dateFormat HH
    title Activity Selection (pick most, no overlap)
    section Activities
    A1 (selected) :done, 01, 4h
    A2            :crit, 02, 5h
    A3 (selected) :done, 05, 3h
    A4            :crit, 06, 4h
    A5 (selected) :done, 08, 2h
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-warning}
Greedy does **not** always work. For general coin change (arbitrary coins), use **DP**.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Dijkstra's Shortest Path {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Finds the **shortest path** from a source node to all other nodes in a **weighted graph** with non-negative edges.

Uses a **min-heap (priority queue)**.

::: {.fragment}
```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-18"}
import heapq

def dijkstra(graph, src):
    dist = {node: float('inf') for node in graph}
    dist[src] = 0
    heap = [(0, src)]     # (distance, node)

    while heap:
        d, u = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if d > dist[u]:
            continue      # stale entry
        for v, w in graph[u]:
            nd = dist[u] + w
            if nd < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = nd
                heapq.heappush(heap, (nd, v))

    return dist
# graph = {'A':[('B',4),('C',2)], ...}
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    A(["A<br/>d=0"]) -->|4| B(["B<br/>d=4"])
    A -->|2| C(["C<br/>d=2"])
    C -->|1| B
    B -->|3| D(["D<br/>d=6"])
    C -->|8| D
    C -->|5| E(["E<br/>d=7"])
    D -->|2| E
    classDef src  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef fin  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class A src
    class B,C,D,E node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Aspect | Value |
|---|---|
| Time (binary heap) | O((V+E) log V) |
| Space | O(V) |
| Negative edges | ❌ (use Bellman-Ford) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Tries — Prefix Trees {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A **Trie** (prefix tree) stores strings character-by-character. Ideal for **autocomplete**, **spell checking**, and **IP routing**.

::: {.fragment}
```python
class TrieNode:
    def __init__(self):
        self.children = {}
        self.is_end   = False

class Trie:
    def __init__(self):
        self.root = TrieNode()

    def insert(self, word):          # O(len)
        node = self.root
        for ch in word:
            if ch not in node.children:
                node.children[ch] = TrieNode()
            node = node.children[ch]
        node.is_end = True

    def search(self, word) -> bool:  # O(len)
        node = self.root
        for ch in word:
            if ch not in node.children:
                return False
            node = node.children[ch]
        return node.is_end
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    ROOT(["root"]) --> CA["c"]
    ROOT --> HA["h"]
    CA --> CAA["a"]
    CAA --> CAT["t ✅"]
    CAA --> CAR["r ✅"]
    HA --> HE["e"]
    HE --> HEL["l"]
    HEL --> HELL["l"]
    HELL --> HELLO["o ✅"]
    HE --> HEA["a"]
    HEA --> HEAP["p ✅"]
    classDef end_ fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class ROOT node
    class HELLO,HEAP,CAT,CAR end_
    class CA,HA,CAA,HE,HEL,HELL,HEA node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Operation | Time |
|---|---|
| Insert | O(L) |
| Search | O(L) |
| Prefix check | O(L) |
| Space | O(ALPHABET × L × N) |

*L = word length, N = number of words*
:::
:::
:::

---

## Space Complexity {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Space complexity** measures how much **extra memory** an algorithm uses relative to input size.

::: {.fragment}
### Common Space Complexities

| Algorithm | Space | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Linear search | O(1) | Only one pointer |
| Binary search | O(1) | Two pointers |
| Merge sort | O(n) | Auxiliary array |
| Quick sort | O(log n) | Call stack |
| BFS / DFS | O(V) | Queue / stack |
| DP knapsack | O(n×W) | 2D table |
| DP knapsack (opt.) | O(W) | Rolling array |
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    O1["O(1) Constant<br/>In-place algorithms<br/>Counters, pointers"]
    OLN["O(log n) Logarithmic<br/>Recursive binary search<br/>Quick sort call stack"]
    ON["O(n) Linear<br/>Auxiliary arrays<br/>Memo tables, hash maps"]
    ON2["O(n²) Quadratic<br/>DP tables<br/>Adjacency matrix"]
    O1 --> OLN --> ON --> ON2
    classDef g fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef y fill:#7a6000,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef r fill:#7a1a1a,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class O1,OLN g
    class ON y
    class ON2 r
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
**Auxiliary space** vs **total space** — Big-O for space usually refers to auxiliary (extra) space, not including the input itself.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Python Collections Cheatsheet {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Python's standard library provides efficient data structure implementations:

::: {.fragment}
```python
from collections import deque, defaultdict, Counter, OrderedDict
import heapq

# deque — O(1) both ends
dq = deque([1, 2, 3])
dq.appendleft(0)    # [0,1,2,3]
dq.popleft()        # O(1)

# defaultdict — auto-initialise missing keys
dd = defaultdict(list)
dd["a"].append(1)   # no KeyError

# Counter — frequency counting
c = Counter("abracadabra")
c.most_common(3)    # [('a',5),('b',2),('r',2)]

# heapq — min-heap
h = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
heapq.heapify(h)    # O(n) in-place
heapq.heappush(h, 2)
heapq.heappop(h)    # smallest
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
### When to Use What

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    Need["What do you need?"]
    Need --> KV["Key → Value<br/>mapping?"]
    Need --> Order["Ordered<br/>sequence?"]
    Need --> Unique["Unique<br/>elements?"]
    Need --> Priority["Priority-based<br/>retrieval?"]
    KV --> DICT["dict / defaultdict"]
    Order --> LIST["list / deque"]
    Unique --> SET["set / frozenset"]
    Priority --> HEAP["heapq"]
    classDef q    fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef ans  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class Need,KV,Order,Unique,Priority q
    class DICT,LIST,SET,HEAP ans
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Algorithm Design Paradigms {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'flowchart': {'rankSpacing': 60}}}%%
graph LR
    PDG["Algorithm<br/>Design Paradigms"]
    PDG --> DC["Divide & Conquer<br/>Split → Solve → Merge<br/>Merge Sort · Quick Sort<br/>Binary Search"]
    PDG --> GR["Greedy<br/>Locallyoptimal choice<br/>Dijkstra · Prim<br/>Huffman · Activity Sel."]
    PDG --> DP2["Dynamic Programming<br/>Memo + Tabulation<br/>Knapsack · LCS · LIS<br/>Edit Distance · Coin Chg"]
    PDG --> BT["Backtracking<br/>Explore all paths<br/>N-Queens · Sudoku<br/>Permutations · Subsets"]
    PDG --> TW["Two Pointers<br/>Left+Right or Slow+Fast<br/>Palindrome · 2-Sum<br/>Linked List Cycle"]
    PDG --> SW["Sliding Window<br/>Subarray / Substring<br/>Max Sum Subarray<br/>Longest no-repeat"]
    classDef root fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef para fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class PDG root
    class DC,GR,DP2,BT,TW,SW para
```

::: {.fragment}
> Recognising **which paradigm** applies is the key skill in solving DSA problems.
:::

---

## Two Pointers & Sliding Window {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
### Two Pointers
Use two indices moving toward each other or at different speeds.

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Two Sum (sorted array) — O(n)
def two_sum_sorted(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo < hi:
        s = arr[lo] + arr[hi]
        if   s == target: return (lo, hi)
        elif s < target:  lo += 1
        else:             hi -= 1
    return None
```
:::

### Sliding Window

::: {.fragment}
```python
# Max sum subarray of size k — O(n)
def max_sum_window(arr, k):
    win = sum(arr[:k])
    best = win
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        win  += arr[i] - arr[i-k]
        best  = max(best, win)
    return best
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "Two Pointers — converging"
        direction LR
        L(["L →"]) --- A1[1] --- A2[2] --- A3[5] --- A4[8] --- A5[9]
        A5 --- R(["← R"])
    end
    subgraph "Sliding Window — fixed size k=3"
        direction LR
        W1["[2  3  5]  4  1"] --> W2["2  [3  5  4]  1"] --> W3["2  3  [5  4  1]"]
    end
    classDef ptr  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef win  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class L,R ptr
    class W1,W2,W3 win
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Complexity Cheatsheet {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Data Structures

| Structure | Access | Search | Insert | Delete |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Array | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(n) |
| Dynamic Array | O(1) | O(n) | O(1)* | O(n) |
| Linked List | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | O(1)† |
| Stack | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Queue | O(n) | O(n) | O(1) | O(1) |
| Hash Table | — | O(1)* | O(1)* | O(1)* |
| BST | O(log n)* | O(log n)* | O(log n)* | O(log n)* |
| Heap | — | O(n) | O(log n) | O(log n) |

*amortised / average · †given node pointer
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Sorting Algorithms

| Algorithm | Best | Avg | Worst | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bubble | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) |
| Insertion | O(n) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) |
| Selection | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(n²) | O(1) |
| Merge | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n) |
| Quick | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n²) | O(log n) |
| Heap | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(n log n) | O(1) |
| Counting | O(n+k) | O(n+k) | O(n+k) | O(k) |

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
Python's built-in `list.sort()` uses **Timsort** — O(n log n) worst case, O(n) nearly-sorted.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Summary & Learning Path {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'flowchart': {'rankSpacing': 55, 'nodeSpacing': 35}}}%%
graph LR
    START(["🚀 Start"]) --> BEGINNER
    subgraph BEGINNER["🟢 Foundations"]
        B1["Big-O Notation"] --> B2["Arrays & Strings"]
        B2 --> B3["Linked Lists"]
        B3 --> B4["Stacks & Queues"]
    end
    BEGINNER --> INTER
    subgraph INTER["🟡 Intermediate"]
        I1["Hash Tables"] --> I2["Trees & BST"]
        I2 --> I3["Heaps & Tries"]
        I3 --> I4["Sorting Algorithms"]
    end
    INTER --> ADV
    subgraph ADV["🔴 Advanced"]
        A1["Graphs<br/>BFS · DFS"] --> A2["Dynamic<br/>Programming"]
        A2 --> A3["Greedy &<br/>Backtracking"]
        A3 --> A4["Dijkstra ·<br/>System Design"]
    end
    ADV --> EXPERT(["🏆 Interview Ready"])
    classDef start  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef end_   fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef beg    fill:#0f3460,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    classDef int_   fill:#16213e,stroke:#f5a623,color:#fff
    classDef adv    fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class START start
    class EXPERT end_
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 beg
    class I1,I2,I3,I4 int_
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 adv
```

::: {.fragment}
> **Practice beats theory.** Solve 2–3 problems per topic on LeetCode / HackerRank, then revisit complexity until it becomes instinct.
:::

---

## DSA Patterns — The 18 Universal Templates {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark', 'flowchart': {'rankSpacing': 50, 'nodeSpacing': 30}}}%%
graph LR
    P["🗂 DSA<br/>Patterns"]
    P --> A["Arrays / Strings"]
    P --> B["Linked Lists"]
    P --> C["Trees / Graphs"]
    P --> D["Intervals / DP"]
    A --> A1["Two Pointers"]
    A --> A2["Sliding Window"]
    A --> A3["Prefix Sum"]
    A --> A4["Monotonic Stack"]
    A --> A5["Top-K Elements"]
    B --> B1["Fast & Slow<br/>Pointers"]
    B --> B2["In-place<br/>List Reversal"]
    C --> C1["Tree BFS<br/>(Level Order)"]
    C --> C2["Tree DFS<br/>(Pre/In/Post)"]
    C --> C3["Topological Sort"]
    C --> C4["Union-Find"]
    C --> C5["Modified<br/>Binary Search"]
    D --> D1["Merge Intervals"]
    D --> D2["Cyclic Sort"]
    D --> D3["0-1 Knapsack<br/>(DP)"]
    D --> D4["Two Heaps"]
    D --> D5["K-way Merge"]
    classDef root fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef cat  fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#16213e,stroke:#555,color:#ccc
    class P root
    class A,B,C,D cat
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,B1,B2,C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 leaf
```

---

## Pattern 1 — Two Pointers {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Move two indices through an array — often **converging** (left←→right) or **same-direction** (slow/fast).

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *pair, triplet, sorted array, palindrome, two-sum*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-9|11-19"}
# ── Converging: Two Sum II (sorted)
def two_sum_sorted(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo < hi:
        s = arr[lo] + arr[hi]
        if   s == target: return [lo, hi]
        elif s < target:  lo += 1
        else:             hi -= 1
    return []

# ── Same-direction: Remove Duplicates
def remove_duplicates(arr):
    if not arr: return 0
    slow = 0
    for fast in range(1, len(arr)):
        if arr[fast] != arr[slow]:
            slow += 1
            arr[slow] = arr[fast]
    return slow + 1
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "Converging — find pair summing to 9"
        direction LR
        L(["L=0"]) --> A1["1"] --- A2["2"] --- A3["4"] --- A4["6"] --- A5["8"]
        A5 --> R(["R=4"])
    end
    subgraph "Same-direction — slow writes, fast reads"
        direction LR
        S(["slow"]) --> B1["1"] --- B2["1"] --- B3["2"] --- B4["3"] --- B5["3"]
        B3 --> F(["fast"])
    end
    classDef ptr fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class L,R,S,F ptr
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Variant | Typical Complexity |
|---|---|
| Converging | O(n) time · O(1) space |
| Same-direction | O(n) time · O(1) space |
| 3-Sum | O(n²) time · O(1) space |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 2 — Sliding Window {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Maintain a **window** (subarray / substring) that grows and shrinks to satisfy a constraint. Avoids recomputing from scratch.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *subarray, substring, contiguous, max/min of k elements*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-10|12-23"}
# ── Fixed window: Max sum of k elements
def max_sum_k(arr, k):
    win  = sum(arr[:k])
    best = win
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        win  += arr[i] - arr[i - k]
        best  = max(best, win)
    return best

# ── Variable window: Longest substring, no repeat
def length_of_longest(s):
    seen = {}
    best = lo = 0
    for hi, ch in enumerate(s):
        if ch in seen and seen[ch] >= lo:
            lo = seen[ch] + 1    # shrink left
        seen[ch] = hi
        best = max(best, hi - lo + 1)
    return best

print(length_of_longest("abcabcbb"))  # 3
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "Fixed window k=3 sliding right"
        direction LR
        W1["[2  3  5] 4  1  6"] --> W2["2 [3  5  4] 1  6"] --> W3["2  3 [5  4  1] 6"] --> W4["2  3  5 [4  1  6]"]
    end
    classDef win fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class W1,W2,W3,W4 win
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
### Variable window template

```python
lo = 0
for hi in range(len(arr)):
    # expand: include arr[hi]
    while <window_invalid>:
        # shrink: exclude arr[lo]
        lo += 1
    # record best window [lo..hi]
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 3 — Fast & Slow Pointers {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Two pointers advance at **different speeds** through a linked list or array. The fast pointer moves 2× as fast.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *cycle detection, middle of list, nth from end*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-11|13-23"}
# ── Detect cycle (Floyd's algorithm)
def has_cycle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
        if slow is fast:
            return True   # cycle found
    return False

# ── Find middle of linked list
def find_middle(head):
    slow = fast = head
    while fast and fast.next:
        slow = slow.next
        fast = fast.next.next
    return slow   # slow is at middle
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    H(["head"]) --> N1["1"] --> N2["2"] --> N3["3"] --> N4["4"] --> N5["5"]
    N5 -->|cycle| N3
    S1(["slow<br/>step 1"]) -.-> N2
    F1(["fast<br/>step 2"]) -.-> N3
    classDef ptr fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef h fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class S1,F1 ptr
    class H h
    class N1,N2,N3,N4,N5 node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Use Case | How |
|---|---|
| Cycle detection | slow==fast → cycle |
| Cycle start | reset slow to head, move both 1 step |
| Middle node | slow stops at middle |
| Nth from end | fast advances N steps first |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 4 — In-place Linked List Reversal {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Reverse a linked list (or a sub-section) **in-place** using three pointers: `prev`, `curr`, `next`.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *reverse a list, reverse between positions, k-group reversal*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-10|12-21"}
# ── Reverse entire linked list
def reverse_list(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        nxt       = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev      = curr
        curr      = nxt
    return prev   # new head

# ── Reverse sublist [left..right]
def reverse_between(head, left, right):
    dummy = ListNode(0, head)
    pre   = dummy
    for _ in range(left - 1):
        pre = pre.next
    cur = pre.next
    for _ in range(right - left):
        nxt       = cur.next
        cur.next  = nxt.next
        nxt.next  = pre.next
        pre.next  = nxt
    return dummy.next
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "Before"
        direction LR
        P1(["prev=None"]) -.-> A1
        A1["1"] --> A2["2"] --> A3["3"] --> A4["4"] --> A5["None"]
        C1(["curr"]) -.-> A1
    end
    subgraph "After"
        direction LR
        B5["None"] --> B4["1"] --> B3["2"] --> B2["3"] --> B1["4"]
        NH(["new head"]) -.-> B1
    end
    classDef ptr  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class P1,C1,NH ptr
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,B1,B2,B3,B4 node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
Always attach a **dummy head** node before position 1 to handle the edge case of reversing from the beginning of the list.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 5 — Tree BFS / Level-Order {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Process a tree **level by level** using a queue. Captures all nodes at the same depth together.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *level order, zigzag, right side view, minimum depth, connect level nodes*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-4|6-16"}
from collections import deque

def level_order(root):
    if not root: return []
    result = []
    queue  = deque([root])

    while queue:
        level = []
        for _ in range(len(queue)):   # snapshot size
            node = queue.popleft()
            level.append(node.val)
            if node.left:  queue.append(node.left)
            if node.right: queue.append(node.right)
        result.append(level)

    return result
# [[1], [2,3], [4,5,6,7]]
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    L0["Level 0: [1]"]
    L1["Level 1: [2, 3]"]
    L2["Level 2: [4, 5, 6, 7]"]
    N1["1"] --> N2["2"]
    N1 --> N3["3"]
    N2 --> N4["4"]
    N2 --> N5["5"]
    N3 --> N6["6"]
    N3 --> N7["7"]
    classDef l0 fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef l1 fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef l2 fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    class N1 l0
    class N2,N3 l1
    class N4,N5,N6,N7 l2
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Common variants:**

- Zigzag level order (alternate direction)
- Right/Left side view (last node per level)
- Minimum depth (first leaf found)
- Average of levels
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 6 — Tree DFS {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Traverse a tree recursively (or with a stack) exploring **all the way down** before backtracking.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *path sum, max depth, validate BST, diameter, lowest common ancestor*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-10|12-22"}
# ── Has Root-to-Leaf path summing to target
def has_path_sum(root, target):
    if not root:
        return False
    if not root.left and not root.right:
        return root.val == target
    rem = target - root.val
    return has_path_sum(root.left,  rem) \
        or has_path_sum(root.right, rem)

# ── All root-to-leaf paths
def all_paths(root):
    paths, path = [], []
    def dfs(node):
        if not node: return
        path.append(node.val)
        if not node.left and not node.right:
            paths.append(list(path))
        dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right)
        path.pop()
    dfs(root)
    return paths
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    R["5"] --> A["4"]
    R --> B["8"]
    A --> C["11"]
    C --> D["7"]
    C --> E["2"]
    B --> F["13"]
    B --> G["4"]
    G --> H["1"]
    D -.->|"path 5→4→11→7<br/>sum=27"| RES(["target=22<br/>❌"])
    E -.->|"path 5→4→11→2<br/>sum=22"| RES2(["target=22<br/>✅"])
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef yes  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef no   fill:#7a1a1a,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class R,A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H node
    class RES2 yes
    class RES no
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 7 — Modified Binary Search {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Adapt binary search for **rotated arrays, unknown size, finding conditions** — not just exact value search.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *sorted (or rotated) array, find first/last occurrence, peak element, search condition*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-9|11-22"}
# ── Find first position of target (leftmost)
def find_first(arr, target):
    lo, hi, result = 0, len(arr)-1, -1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target:
            result = mid; hi = mid - 1   # keep going left
        elif arr[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else:                   hi = mid - 1
    return result

# ── Search in rotated sorted array
def search_rotated(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target: return mid
        if arr[lo] <= arr[mid]:     # left half sorted
            if arr[lo] <= target < arr[mid]: hi = mid - 1
            else:                            lo = mid + 1
        else:                       # right half sorted
            if arr[mid] < target <= arr[hi]: lo = mid + 1
            else:                            hi = mid - 1
    return -1
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    ROT["Rotated array<br/>[4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2]"]
    ROT --> Q["mid = arr[3] = 7<br/>target = 0"]
    Q --> LEFT["Left half [4..7]<br/>sorted? YES"]
    Q --> RIGHT["Right half [0..2]<br/>target 0 in here?"]
    RIGHT --> NARROW["Narrow to right half<br/>lo = mid+1"]
    classDef step fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef dec  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class ROT,Q,LEFT,RIGHT,NARROW step
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Template variations:**

| Problem | Adjust |
|---|---|
| First/last occurrence | record `mid`, keep searching |
| Peak element | compare `mid` with `mid+1` |
| Min in rotated array | find inflection point |
| Search in matrix | treat 2D as 1D |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 8 — Top-K Elements (Heap) {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Use a **heap of size K** to efficiently track the top K elements — avoiding a full sort.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *top K, K largest/smallest, K closest, K most frequent*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-10|12-21"}
import heapq

# ── K largest elements — min-heap of size K
def k_largest(arr, k):
    heap = arr[:k]
    heapq.heapify(heap)                  # O(k)
    for num in arr[k:]:
        if num > heap[0]:
            heapq.heapreplace(heap, num) # O(log k)
    return heap                          # total O(n log k)

# ── K most frequent elements
from collections import Counter

def top_k_frequent(nums, k):
    freq  = Counter(nums)
    return heapq.nlargest(k, freq, key=freq.get)

print(top_k_frequent([1,1,1,2,2,3], 2))  # [1, 2]
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    IN["nums = [3,1,5,12,2,11]<br/>k = 3"] --> HEAP["Min-Heap (size k=3)<br/>[3, 1, 5]"]
    HEAP --> C1["num=12 > heap[0]=1<br/>replace → [3, 5, 12]"]
    C1 --> C2["num=2 < heap[0]=3<br/>skip"]
    C2 --> C3["num=11 > heap[0]=3<br/>replace → [5, 12, 11]"]
    C3 --> OUT(["Result: [5, 11, 12]"])
    classDef step fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef out  fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class IN,HEAP,C1,C2,C3 step
    class OUT out
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Approach | Time | Space |
|---|---|---|
| Sort + slice | O(n log n) | O(1) |
| Min-heap size K | **O(n log k)** | O(k) |
| Quickselect | O(n) avg | O(1) |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 9 — Merge Intervals {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Sort intervals by start time, then greedily merge overlapping ones.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *intervals, overlapping, meeting rooms, insert interval, free time*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-4|5-13"}
# ── Merge Overlapping Intervals
def merge(intervals):
    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    merged = [intervals[0]]

    for start, end in intervals[1:]:
        if start <= merged[-1][1]:           # overlap
            merged[-1][1] = max(merged[-1][1], end)
        else:
            merged.append([start, end])      # no overlap

    return merged

print(merge([[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]))
# → [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]]
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
gantt
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Input (sorted)
    [1,3]    :a1, 1, 3
    [2,6]    :a2, 2, 6
    [8,10]   :a3, 8, 10
    [15,18]  :a4, 15, 18
    section Merged
    [1,6]    :done, m1, 1, 6
    [8,10]   :done, m2, 8, 10
    [15,18]  :done, m3, 15, 18
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Common variants:**

| Problem | Key Change |
|---|---|
| Insert interval | binary search insertion point |
| Meeting rooms I | any overlap? |
| Meeting rooms II | min rooms = max overlapping at once |
| Employee free time | merge all, find gaps |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 10 — Cyclic Sort {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
When numbers are in a range **[1..n]** (or [0..n]), each number belongs at a predictable index. Swap elements to their correct positions in **O(n)**.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *missing number, duplicate, first missing positive, numbers in range 1..n*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-10|12-20"}
# ── Cyclic Sort — put each num at index num-1
def cyclic_sort(nums):
    i = 0
    while i < len(nums):
        j = nums[i] - 1            # correct index
        if nums[i] != nums[j]:
            nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
        else:
            i += 1
    return nums

# ── Find Missing Number  [0..n]
def find_missing(nums):
    i = 0
    while i < len(nums):
        j = nums[i]
        if nums[i] < len(nums) and nums[i] != nums[j]:
            nums[i], nums[j] = nums[j], nums[i]
        else:
            i += 1
    for i, num in enumerate(nums):
        if num != i: return i
    return len(nums)
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "nums = [3, 1, 5, 4, 2]"
        direction LR
        S0["[3, 1, 5, 4, 2]"]
        S0 -->|"swap idx0↔idx2"| S1["[5, 1, 3, 4, 2]"]
        S1 -->|"swap idx0↔idx4"| S2["[2, 1, 3, 4, 5]"]
        S2 -->|"swap idx0↔idx1"| S3["[1, 2, 3, 4, 5] ✅"]
    end
    classDef step fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef done fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class S0,S1,S2 step
    class S3 done
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-tip}
After the sort pass, a single scan finds mismatches — revealing **missing** or **duplicate** numbers without extra space.
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 11 — Two Heaps {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Maintain a **max-heap** for the lower half and a **min-heap** for the upper half to efficiently query the **median** in a stream.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *median of a stream, sliding window median, schedule tasks*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-7|9-22"}
import heapq

class MedianFinder:
    def __init__(self):
        self.lo = []   # max-heap (negate values)
        self.hi = []   # min-heap

    def add_num(self, num):
        heapq.heappush(self.lo, -num)      # push to max-heap
        # balance: max(lo) must be ≤ min(hi)
        if self.hi and -self.lo[0] > self.hi[0]:
            heapq.heappush(self.hi, -heapq.heappop(self.lo))
        # size balance: lo may have one extra
        if len(self.lo) > len(self.hi) + 1:
            heapq.heappush(self.hi, -heapq.heappop(self.lo))
        elif len(self.hi) > len(self.lo):
            heapq.heappush(self.lo, -heapq.heappop(self.hi))

    def find_median(self):
        if len(self.lo) > len(self.hi):
            return -self.lo[0]
        return (-self.lo[0] + self.hi[0]) / 2
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    subgraph "Lower half (max-heap)"
        direction TB
        L1["3"] --> L2["2"] --> L3["1"]
    end
    subgraph "Upper half (min-heap)"
        direction TB
        H1["4"] --> H2["5"] --> H3["6"]
    end
    MED(["median = (3+4)/2 = 3.5"])
    L1 -.->|"top of lo"| MED
    H1 -.->|"top of hi"| MED
    classDef lh fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef uh fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    classDef m  fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class L1,L2,L3 lh
    class H1,H2,H3 uh
    class MED m
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 12 — Topological Sort {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Order nodes of a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** such that every edge `u → v` comes before `v`. Uses **in-degree** tracking + BFS (Kahn's algorithm).

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *task scheduling, course prerequisites, build order, dependency resolution*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-7|9-20"}
from collections import deque, defaultdict

def topo_sort(n, prerequisites):
    graph   = defaultdict(list)
    in_deg  = [0] * n
    for a, b in prerequisites:
        graph[b].append(a)      # b must come before a
        in_deg[a] += 1

    queue = deque(i for i in range(n) if in_deg[i] == 0)
    order = []
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        order.append(node)
        for nxt in graph[node]:
            in_deg[nxt] -= 1
            if in_deg[nxt] == 0:
                queue.append(nxt)

    return order if len(order) == n else []  # [] = cycle
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    A["Maths<br/>in-deg 0"] --> C["Algorithms<br/>in-deg 2"]
    B["Python<br/>in-deg 0"] --> C
    B --> D["Data Structures<br/>in-deg 1"]
    D --> C
    C --> E["Interview<br/>Prep"]
    classDef zero fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    classDef wait fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef last fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class A,B zero
    class C,D wait
    class E last
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
Order: A → B → D → C → E  
*If a cycle exists, not all nodes are added → detect it!*
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 13 — Union-Find (Disjoint Set) {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Efficiently **group elements** into disjoint sets with near-O(1) `union` and `find` operations using **path compression** + **union by rank**.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *connected components, cycle detection, number of islands, redundant connection*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-8|10-18|20-26"}
class UnionFind:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.parent = list(range(n))
        self.rank   = [0] * n
        self.count  = n   # number of components

    def find(self, x):            # path compression
        if self.parent[x] != x:
            self.parent[x] = self.find(self.parent[x])
        return self.parent[x]

    def union(self, x, y):        # union by rank
        rx, ry = self.find(x), self.find(y)
        if rx == ry: return False  # already same set
        if self.rank[rx] < self.rank[ry]: rx, ry = ry, rx
        self.parent[ry] = rx
        if self.rank[rx] == self.rank[ry]: self.rank[rx] += 1
        self.count -= 1
        return True
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    subgraph "Before union(3,4)"
        direction LR
        G1["0"] --- G2["1"] --- G3["2"]
        G4["3"]
        G5["4"]
    end
    subgraph "After union(3,4)"
        direction LR
        H1["0"] --- H2["1"] --- H3["2"]
        H4["3"] --- H5["4"]
    end
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    class G1,G2,G3,G4,G5,H1,H2,H3,H4,H5 node
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Operation | Time (amortised) |
|---|---|
| find | O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) |
| union | O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) |
| connected? | O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) |

*(α = inverse Ackermann function)*
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 14 — Prefix Sum {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Precompute **cumulative sums** so any subarray sum is retrieved in **O(1)**.

$$sum(l, r) = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]$$

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *subarray sum, range sum query, number of subarrays with sum k*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-9|11-22"}
# ── Range sum queries in O(1) after O(n) build
def build_prefix(arr):
    prefix = [0] * (len(arr) + 1)
    for i, v in enumerate(arr):
        prefix[i+1] = prefix[i] + v
    return prefix

def range_sum(prefix, l, r):
    return prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]

# ── Count subarrays with sum == k  (hash map)
from collections import defaultdict

def subarray_sum_k(nums, k):
    count   = 0
    running = 0
    seen    = defaultdict(int)
    seen[0] = 1                      # empty prefix
    for num in nums:
        running += num
        count   += seen[running - k] # complement found
        seen[running] += 1
    return count

print(subarray_sum_k([1,1,1], 2))    # → 2
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "arr = [3, 0, -2, 6, 1]"
        direction LR
        A0["3"] --- A1["0"] --- A2["-2"] --- A3["6"] --- A4["1"]
    end
    subgraph "prefix = [0, 3, 3, 1, 7, 8]"
        direction LR
        P0["0"] --- P1["3"] --- P2["3"] --- P3["1"] --- P4["7"] --- P5["8"]
    end
    QUERY(["sum(2,4) = prefix[5]-prefix[2]<br/>= 8-3 = 5"])
    classDef arr   fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef pre   fill:#16213e,stroke:#4ecca3,color:#fff
    classDef query fill:#e94560,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4 arr
    class P0,P1,P2,P3,P4,P5 pre
    class QUERY query
```
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 15 — Monotonic Stack {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Maintain a stack whose elements are always **monotonically increasing or decreasing**. Used to find **next greater/smaller** elements in O(n).

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *next greater element, daily temperatures, largest rectangle, trapping rain water*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-12|14-22"}
# ── Next Greater Element  O(n)
def next_greater(nums):
    result = [-1] * len(nums)
    stack  = []           # stores indices, decreasing values

    for i, val in enumerate(nums):
        while stack and nums[stack[-1]] < val:
            idx          = stack.pop()
            result[idx]  = val   # val is the next greater
        stack.append(i)

    return result         # remaining stack entries stay -1

# ── Daily Temperatures
def daily_temps(T):
    result = [0] * len(T)
    stack  = []
    for i, temp in enumerate(T):
        while stack and T[stack[-1]] < temp:
            j          = stack.pop()
            result[j]  = i - j
        stack.append(i)
    return result
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph LR
    subgraph "nums = [2, 1, 5, 3, 6]"
        direction LR
        N0["2"] --- N1["1"] --- N2["5"] --- N3["3"] --- N4["6"]
    end
    subgraph "Next Greater Result"
        direction LR
        R0["5"] --- R1["5"] --- R2["6"] --- R3["6"] --- R4["-1"]
    end
    classDef inp fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef res fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class N0,N1,N2,N3,N4 inp
    class R0,R1,R2,R3,R4 res
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Stack type | Solves |
|---|---|
| Decreasing (pop on larger) | Next Greater Element |
| Increasing (pop on smaller) | Next Smaller Element |
| Decreasing with area calc | Largest Rectangle |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 16 — 0/1 Knapsack DP Family {.scrollable background-color="#16213e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
A family of DP problems where you either **take or skip** an item each step. Recognise by the binary choice at each element.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *subset sum, 0/1 knapsack, partition equal subset, target sum (count ways)*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-11|13-23"}
# ── Subset Sum — does a subset sum to target?
def can_partition(nums, target):
    dp = {0}                          # reachable sums
    for num in nums:
        dp |= {s + num for s in dp}   # take or skip
    return target in dp

# ── Count ways to reach target
def count_ways(nums, target):
    dp = [0] * (target + 1)
    dp[0] = 1                         # 1 way to sum to 0
    for num in nums:
        for t in range(target, num - 1, -1):  # right→left!
            dp[t] += dp[t - num]
    return dp[target]

print(can_partition([1,5,11,5], 11))  # True
print(count_ways([1,2,3], 4))         # 4
# (1+1+2, 1+3, 1+1+1+1, 2+2)
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    ROOT["dp[0..W] = [T,F,F,F,F,F]"]
    ROOT --> R1["After num=1<br/>[T,T,F,F,F,F]"]
    R1 --> R2["After num=5<br/>[T,T,F,F,F,T,T]"]
    R2 --> R3["After num=11<br/>[T,T,F,F,F,T,T,...,T]"]
    R3 --> DONE(["dp[11] = True ✅"])
    classDef step fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef done fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class ROOT,R1,R2,R3 step
    class DONE done
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Key insight:** iterate `target` in **reverse** direction to prevent using the same item twice (0/1 constraint).
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 17 — Backtracking {.scrollable background-color="#0f3460"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Explore **all possibilities** by building candidates incrementally and **abandoning** (backtracking) as soon as a constraint is violated.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *all subsets, all permutations, combinations, N-Queens, Sudoku, word search*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-18"}
# ── Template
def backtrack(path, choices):
    if <is_solution>:
        result.append(list(path)); return
    for choice in choices:
        if <is_valid(choice)>:
            path.append(choice)        # make choice
            backtrack(path, next_choices)
            path.pop()                 # undo choice

# ── All subsets of nums
def subsets(nums):
    result, path = [], []
    def bt(start):
        result.append(list(path))
        for i in range(start, len(nums)):
            path.append(nums[i]); bt(i + 1); path.pop()
    bt(0)
    return result   # 2^n subsets
```
:::
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.fragment}
```{mermaid}
%%{init: {'theme': 'dark'}}%%
graph TD
    ROOT(["[]"]) --> A(["[1]"])
    ROOT --> B(["[2]"])
    ROOT --> C(["[3]"])
    A --> AB(["[1,2]"])
    A --> AC(["[1,3]"])
    AB --> ABC(["[1,2,3]"])
    B --> BC(["[2,3]"])
    classDef node fill:#0f3460,stroke:#e94560,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#1a6b3c,stroke:none,color:#fff
    class ROOT,A,B,C node
    class AB,AC,ABC,BC leaf
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Problem | Pruning condition |
|---|---|
| Subsets | none (explore all) |
| Permutations | skip already used |
| Combinations of sum | `remaining < 0` |
| N-Queens | row/col/diag conflict |
:::
:::
:::

---

## Pattern 18 — K-way Merge {.scrollable background-color="#1a1a2e"}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
Merge **K sorted lists** (or arrays) using a **min-heap** that always yields the globally smallest element next.

::: {.fragment}
**Signal words:** *merge K sorted lists/arrays, smallest range covering K lists, K closest elements*

```{.python code-line-numbers="1-5|7-19"}
import heapq

class ListNode:
```
